Blank stare followed by head version and ipsilateral arm extension, most commonly to the right (rightward head version with right arm extension), occasionally to the left (leftward head version with left arm extension); progression to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with loss of responsiveness and postural control; rhythmic jerks observed in some episodes. Blank stare with or without automatisms such as lip smacking, jaw clenching, or subtle head movements, initially unresponsive; transitions to lateralizing features including rightward or leftward head version with ipsilateral arm extension. This is followed by bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by rhythmic jerking of arms and legs, often accompanied by jaw clenching, tongue protrusion, or loss of postural control. The patient remains unresponsive throughout, with preserved symmetric involvement in some episodes, and consistently demonstrates impaired consciousness or loss of responsiveness at termination.

Blank stare, often with lip smacking or mild rightward head turn, progressing to definite rightward head version with right arm extension; subsequently evolving into bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by rhythmic, vigorous jerking of limbs, jaw clenching, and loss of postural control. Blank stare, followed by rightward head version with right arm extension and left arm flexion in some instances; progression to bilateral tonic–clonic movements with rhythmic clonic jerks observed across episodes. Loss of posturing and unresponsiveness noted at the conclusion of events.

Blank stare, often accompanied by lip smacking, rhythmic jaw movements, or upward gaze, consistently transitions to rightward head version with right arm extension. In some instances, left arm flexion accompanies right arm extension before bilateral spread. This initial phase evolves into bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by limb jerking, jaw clenching, and loss of postural tone or control. Unresponsiveness is uniformly present during the later stages. Rhythmic clonic jerks become generalized, and all episodes culminate in a state of unresponsiveness with sustained motor convulsions.

Blank stare typically evolves with focal features including rightward or leftward head version, upward gaze, or orofacial automatisms such as lip smacking; rightward head deviation with right arm extension is the most frequently observed focal onset. This may be accompanied by ipsilateral limb posturing or flexion, and in some cases contralateral progression (e.g., leftward head version with left arm flexion). The initial focal phase consistently progresses to bilateral tonic–clonic movements characterized by symmetric or asymmetric limb jerking, tonic stiffening, clonic jerking, jaw clenching, and limb rigidity or extension. Loss of awareness, unresponsiveness, or loss of consciousness is universal during the event, with no automatisms observed following the onset of bilateral convulsive activity. In a minority of instances, the blank stare progresses directly to bilateral tonic–clonic movements without clear lateralized onset, and without discernible automatisms or focal features. Postural control is lost during generalized convulsive phases.
Blank stare, then rightward head version with right arm extension; later bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Unresponsive at the end.

Blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, with variable left arm flexion; rhythmic clonic jerks initially involving one or both limbs progress to bilateral tonic–clonic movements. Loss of responsiveness and postural control commonly occur during the evolving seizure phase. Some episodes begin with subtle rightward head rotation and asymmetric limb involvement before generalizing.
